Psalm 68 confronts us with a God who is far more active and powerful than we often dare imagine. This psalm sweeps across history, showing us the Lord arising to scatter His enemies like smoke, marching through the wilderness to save His people, and commanding all creation to respond. Yet embedded within this cosmic display of power is something startling: this same God who crushes the heads of His enemies is also "a father of the fatherless and a defender of widows." He settles the lonely in families and leads prisoners out to prosperity. The psalmist does not soften God's power or minimize His judgment against the wicked—the imagery is fierce and uncompromising. But neither does He allow God's strength to be separated from His tender care for the vulnerable. We are forced to reckon with the full weight of who God actually is: neither a distant cosmic power indifferent to suffering, nor a sentimental deity unwilling to judge evil, but the one Lord who is both the refuge of the broken and the terror of those who rebel against Him.

What arrests us in this psalm is the movement of God Himself. He does not remain static in heaven, aloof from His people's struggle. He arises, He marches, He sends rain, He ascends in triumph, He processes into His sanctuary. The church sings and celebrates in response—first the maidens with tambourines, then the gathered tribes of Israel, then all the kingdoms of the earth. This is not passive worship offered from safe distance, but a response to God's active intervention in history. The psalmist looks back to Sinai, where God revealed His power at the giving of the Law, and forward to a day when all nations will bring gifts to His temple and submit to His rule. Between these moments lies our present, where we live in a world not yet fully submitted to God's reign, yet where His strength and His mercy are real and active right now.

The psalm closes with a remarkable call: "Ascribe the power to God." Not power that we conjure or imagine, but power that He possesses and has already demonstrated. The God of Israel gives "strength and power to His people"—not the strength to rule others, but the strength to stand when enemies gather, to survive when scattered, to endure when tested. This is why the psalm can both warn of God's terrible judgment and invite the vulnerable to find refuge in Him. He crushes His enemies and defends the defenseless with the same authentic power. As you read this psalm, do not rush past the difficult passages or retreat into comfortable abstractions about power and love. Let the full reality of who God is—His active, unstoppable, redemptive strength—sink into you. This is the God who encounters you today.
